explicit_text: I was born in Tacoma, Washington on September 30, 1942. My birth parents lived there before the war, but during World War II, my mother was relocated away from our home in Tacoma due to the internment policies. It was a difficult time for my family, as my father was deported to Japan and my mother was left to care for our children alone.
implicit_text: I was born in a city in Washington state on September 30, 1942. My birth parents lived there before the war, but during World War II, my mother was relocated away from our home due to the internment policies. It was a difficult time for my family, as my father was deported to his country of origin and my mother was left to care for our children alone.

explicit_text: I lived in the Children's Village at Manzanar as an infant after my mother gave me up for adoption during the war. The care there was excellent, thanks to Mrs. Matsumoto and the dedicated volunteers, and notably, no children died in the camp. My legal adoption was processed through the Manzanar social worker, Ms. Robbins, in 1946.
implicit_text: I lived in the children's section of a California relocation center as an infant after my mother gave me up for adoption during the war. The care there was excellent, thanks to Mrs. Matsumoto and the dedicated volunteers, and notably, no children died in the camp. My legal adoption was processed through the social worker at that center, Ms. Robbins, in 1946.

explicit_text: Manzanar was the internment camp where I spent my earliest years after being born during World War II. Although I was too young to remember much, I know that the Children's Village there provided excellent care. It was a place where many Japanese Americans, including my family, were forcibly relocated during the war.
implicit_text: The California relocation center was where I spent my earliest years after being born during World War II. Although I was too young to remember much, I know that the children's section there provided excellent care. It was a place where many Japanese Americans, including my family, were forcibly relocated during the war.

explicit_text: My birth mother and siblings were sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center after being removed from our home in Tacoma. It was during this time, before arriving at Tule Lake, that my mother gave birth to me and made the difficult decision to offer me up for adoption. Tule Lake was one of the camps where Japanese Americans were confined during the war.
implicit_text: My birth mother and siblings were sent to a northern California war relocation center after being removed from our home in Washington. It was during this time, before arriving at that camp, that my mother gave birth to me and made the difficult decision to offer me up for adoption. That center was one of the camps where Japanese Americans were confined during the war.

explicit_text: My birth father was originally from Japan and came to the United States illegally as a fisherman. After Executive Order 9066 was signed, he was deported back to Japan for nearly ten years during the war. Fortunately, after the war, my family petitioned for his return, and he reunited with my mother and siblings in Ogden, Utah.
implicit_text: My birth father was originally from the island nation in East Asia and came to the United States illegally as a fisherman. After the government order was signed, he was deported back to his homeland for nearly ten years during the war. Fortunately, after the war, my family petitioned for his return, and he reunited with my mother and siblings in a city in Utah.

explicit_text: Executive Order 9066 was the government order that led to the forced removal and internment of Japanese Americans, including my family. It resulted in my father's deportation back to Japan because he was an illegal entrant, and my mother and siblings being relocated to camps like Tule Lake. This order changed the course of our lives during the war.
implicit_text: The government order issued during World War II led to the forced removal and internment of Japanese Americans, including my family. It resulted in my father's deportation back to his country because he was an illegal entrant, and my mother and siblings being relocated to camps like the one in northern California. This order changed the course of our lives during the war.

explicit_text: After the war, my birth mother and siblings moved to Ogden, Utah, where they temporarily lived with friends and began rebuilding their lives. My father also reunited with the family there after returning from Japan. Ogden became an important place for my birth family’s post-war resettlement.
implicit_text: After the war, my birth mother and siblings moved to a city in Utah, where they temporarily lived with friends and began rebuilding their lives. My father also reunited with the family there after returning from his homeland. That city became an important place for my birth family’s post-war resettlement.

explicit_text: My adoptive parents took me and my adopted brother Ken to Yerington, Nevada after the war. They lived there outside the exclusion zone and were not subject to internment. My adoptive father was active in the community, but sadly, his Rotary Club membership was revoked because of his Japanese heritage, which deeply hurt him.
implicit_text: My adoptive parents took me and my adopted brother Ken to a small city in Nevada after the war. They lived there outside the exclusion zone and were not subject to internment. My adoptive father was active in the community, but sadly, his local service club membership was revoked because of his Japanese heritage, which deeply hurt him.

explicit_text: When I was six years old, my family relocated to San Francisco, where I lived for over twenty years. Growing up in San Francisco's Japantown, I experienced a multicultural environment with relatively little discrimination, although I did face some incidents like being called a racial slur once. It was a place where I was able to grow up with a strong sense of community.
implicit_text: When I was six years old, my family relocated to a major city in California, where I lived for over twenty years. Growing up in the city's Japantown, I experienced a multicultural environment with relatively little discrimination, although I did face some incidents like being called a racial slur once. It was a place where I was able to grow up with a strong sense of community.

explicit_text: My adoptive father was a respected member of the Rotary International club in Yerington, Nevada, where he contributed a lot to the community. However, during the war, the club revoked his membership because he was Japanese, which was a devastating blow to him. This experience highlighted the discrimination faced by Japanese Americans even outside the camps.
implicit_text: My adoptive father was a respected member of a global service organization in the Nevada city where we lived, contributing a lot to the community. However, during the war, the club revoked his membership because he was Japanese, which was a devastating blow to him. This experience highlighted the discrimination faced by Japanese Americans even outside the camps.

explicit_text: The United States was both the country I was born in and the place where my family endured the hardships of internment during World War II. Despite the injustices we faced, including forced relocation and discrimination, I have a deep connection to this country. Later in life, I appreciated the government's official apology and reparations for the internment.
implicit_text: This country was both the place I was born and where my family endured the hardships of internment during World War II. Despite the injustices we faced, including forced relocation and discrimination, I have a deep connection to this nation. Later in life, I appreciated the government's official apology and reparations for the internment.

explicit_text: I received a letter from President Bill Clinton as part of the redress for Japanese American internment. His official apology on White House stationary was very significant to me and marked an important day in my life. It symbolized recognition of the injustices my family and others endured during the war.
implicit_text: I received a letter from the president at the time as part of the redress for Japanese American internment. His official apology on White House stationary was very significant to me and marked an important day in my life. It symbolized recognition of the injustices my family and others endured during the war.

explicit_text: George Sakato was a good family friend who served with my uncle in the 442nd Infantry Regiment during World War II. He often reminded us that while we should never forget the past, the future is far more important. His words have stayed with me and reflect the resilience of the Japanese American community.
implicit_text: A good family friend who served with my uncle in a famous Japanese American infantry regiment during World War II often reminded us that while we should never forget the past, the future is far more important. His words have stayed with me and reflect the resilience of the Japanese American community.

explicit_text: The 442nd Infantry Regiment was the unit in which my uncle and George Sakato served during World War II. This regiment is known for its bravery and sacrifice despite the discrimination faced by Japanese Americans at home. Their service is a proud part of my family's history and legacy.
